sections.title: Connect — manifest to a DeepBook-extended client
sections.key_moment: The `new SuiGrpcClient(...).$extend(deepbook({ packageIds, coins, pools }))` construction. The extension is what grafts `client.deepbook.deepBook.*` onto the base client — and its config is *derived from the manifest*: `extractObjectId` matches the `Registry` object while excluding `MarginRegistry`, and the per-coin `scalar` (DEEP 1e6, SUI 1e9) must match each coin's on-chain decimals or every amount is off by orders of magnitude.

sections.title: Fund & execute — a fresh keypair and a balance-aware send
sections.key_moment: `signAndExecute` requesting `include: { effects: true, balanceChanges: true }` and then `waitForTransaction`. The `balanceChanges` flag is load-bearing — section 4 reads the real fill out of it, and if it isn't requested here the data simply isn't there. A fresh `Ed25519Keypair` funded per run is what keeps the live test isolated from prior chain state.

sections.title: Swap — the leftover-coin tuple you must hand back
sections.key_moment: The `const [baseCoin, quoteCoin, deepCoin] = tx.add(swapExactQuoteForBase({...}))` return tuple, followed by the mandatory `tx.transferObjects([baseCoin, quoteCoin, deepCoin], address)`. The builder is a thunk that yields three leftover coin handles; if you don't transfer them back to the sender they are *destroyed* at the end of the transaction. `deepAmount: 0` because the sandbox pools are whitelisted (no DEEP fee).

sections.title: Read the fill — the real base-out from balanceChanges
sections.key_moment: `baseDelta` — sum the *positive* balanceChange of the pool's base coin type credited to the sender, comparing coin types through `normalizeStructTag` (so the manifest type matches the canonicalized on-chain type) and handling both the gRPC `address` and JSON-RPC `owner` owner shapes, then divide by the base scalar. `baseOut` is a genuine on-chain figure, not a placeholder — that is the whole point of reading effects instead of guessing.

sections.title: Drive it — smoke UI and the live slippage gate
sections.key_moment: Wiring `setupSandbox()` → `swapQuoteForBase()` into the minimal driver and running the live suite. The slippage guard is proven by the second test: an unsatisfiable `minOut` makes the pool revert with MoveAbort code 12 (`EMinimumQuantityOutNotMet`) — the test asserts *that specific* abort, not just any throw, which is what proves `minOut` is real on-chain protection rather than a client-side check.
